tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

<blueprint>

extended.description: An address extension such as an apartment number, C/O or alternative name.

locality.description: The locality in which the street address is, and which is in the region.

region.description: The region in which the locality is, and which is in the country.

postOfficeBoxNumber.description: The post office box number for PO Box addresses.

country.description: The country in 2-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 format.

concurrency.description: Maximum number of concurrent invocations of the trigger. For Feed triggers, this will process Feed items in parallel.
concurrency.title: Concurrency

content.text: Let me summarize the available schemas/typenames.

Here are all the available schemas and their typenames:

| # | Typename | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | `com.example.type.expando` | A flexible/generic object type that allows additional properties |
| 2 | `org.dxos.type.function` | Represents a function with name, description, input/output schemas, and service bindings |
| 3 | `org.dxos.type.organization` | An organization with fields like name, description, status, website, location, etc. |
| 4 | `org.dxos.type.person` | A person/contact with fields like full name, emails, phone numbers, addresses, job title, etc. |
| 5 | `org.dxos.type.tag` | A tag with a label and optional hue (color), used for tagging/categorizing objects |
| 6 | `org.dxos.type.trigger` | A trigger configuration that links to a function and defines when it should fire (email, queue, subscription, timer, or webhook) |

These are the six typenames currently available in the space. Let me know if you'd like more details on any specific schema or want to create/query objects of a particular type!

# DXOS — The Decentralized Operating System

## Overview

<cite index="1-3">DXOS is an open source framework for building real-time, collaborative web applications that run entirely on the client and communicate peer-to-peer, without the need for centralized servers.</cite> The acronym stands for **Decentralized Operating System**. <cite index="6-3,6-4">It is a decentralized platform designed to create the first functionally and commercially competitive, fully decentralized alternative to the corporate cloud, offering a network operating system, infrastructure platform, programming framework, and toolchain, enabling businesses to develop privacy-preserving applications for its users and a fairer marketplace for open-source developers.</cite>

## Company Background

- **Founded:** <cite index="6-5">DXOS was founded in 2021.</cite>
- **Headquarters:** <cite index="6-6">DXOS is headquartered in New York, NY.</cite>
- **Status:** <cite index="6-8">DXOS is a private company.</cite>
- **Team:** <cite index="3-6">DXOS was created by a small core team of developers from many countries.</cite>
- **Community:** <cite index="31-5">The community spans 8 cities worldwide, has 30+ contributors and 200+ community members.</cite>
- **Investors:** <cite index="6-10">Backed VC, BlueYard Capital, Empty Set Group, and PL Capital (United States) have invested in DXOS.</cite>

## Mission

<cite index="20-2">DXOS's mission is to help developers build privacy-preserving, self-sovereign software.</cite> <cite index="1-21,1-22">They're setting a new standard for how modern software is built by reducing reliance on centralized servers and focusing on scalable peer-to-peer data networks. Their framework empowers anyone to create real-time collaborative tools within a privacy-focused, local-first workspace that is enhanced by AI capabilities.</cite>

## Core Technology Stack

The DXOS SDK is composed of three main technology pillars:

### 1. ECHO (Database)
<cite index="1-6">ECHO is an open source database architecture that incorporates transparent data replication and conflict resolution for secure and scalable local storage.</cite> <cite index="12-8">It is a peer-to-peer database and reactive state container for offline-first, real-time, collaborative apps.</cite>

### 2. HALO (Identity)
<cite index="1-7">HALO seamlessly manages digital identities using public/private key pairs allowing nuanced control over access to applications, databases, and networked devices.</cite> <cite index="5-5">HALO is a decentralized identity and access control protocol that makes it easier to build collaborative applications.</cite>

### 3. MESH (Networking)
<cite index="1-9">MESH extends existing internet protocols to enable secure and resilient peer-to-peer networks without the need for centralized server infrastructure.</cite> <cite index="1-19">Data is automatically synchronized across networked devices using WebRTC for cloudless peer-to-peer networking.</cite>

### Underlying Data Model (CRDTs / Automerge)
<cite index="1-11,1-12">DXOS leverages Automerge — a powerful CRDT that enables multiple concurrent users to operate on the same data without loss or conflict.</cite>

## Architecture Principles

- **Local-First:** <cite index="1-14,1-15">The only reason you need a central server is to serve your application shell. Everything else happens in the client and works peer-to-peer.</cite>
- **Privacy by Default:** <cite index="5-6">There are still signaling servers for peer discovery, but all data exchange happens peer-to-peer, and no servers have access to the data.</cite>
- **Offline Support:** <cite index="14-2">Supports offline functionality with automatic synchronization upon reconnection.</cite>
- **Open Source:** <cite index="36-5">Open-source under the MIT license.</cite>

## SDKs and Developer Tools

<cite index="3-12">DXOS wraps its three main components together in a set of SDKs (currently, React and TypeScript) that enable developers to deliver sophisticated applications quickly.</cite> The project is written in TypeScript and <cite index="2-2">has 64 repositories available on GitHub.</cite>

<cite index="12-9">The SDK also includes an App Framework to build modular applications based around plugins and capabilities, such as Composer.</cite>

## Flagship Application: Composer

<cite index="4-6">To show off the power of DXOS, they've built Composer: a local-first workspace that is designed for small team collaboration.</cite>

<cite index="33-10">Composer is a collaborative productivity app where developers can build and organize knowledge, extend with custom data and UI, and run private AI against their knowledge locally.</cite>

### Composer Features:
- <cite index="31-14">Composer blends the power of popular file formats (docs, sheets, sketch & tables) while allowing you to build plugins, deploy custom functions and leverage modern LLMs.</cite>
- <cite index="31-15">Realtime collaboration inside of a modern markdown editor.</cite>
- <cite index="31-17">Threaded comments across multiple document formats.</cite>
- <cite index="31-19">Custom plugins to enhance functionality.</cite>
- <cite index="31-21">Custom schemas to work with structured data.</cite>
- <cite index="31-23">Local-first spreadsheet that supports custom functions.</cite>
- <cite index="31-27">Machine learning to build agentic workflows that enhance team productivity while protecting intellectual property.</cite>
- <cite index="31-49">Import and export of the entire database as plain, human-readable markdown, JSON, and media files.</cite>

### Composer's Design Pillars:
- **Extensibility:** <cite index="33-11,33-12">If a team is operating in a challenging problem domain, they need knowledge tools specific to their work. Composer is built for customization from the ground up.</cite>
- **Collaboration:** <cite index="33-13,33-14">Composer has real-time multiplayer implemented through a local-first model, making a lot of things better: from meeting notes to working on airplanes.</cite>
- **Openness:** <cite index="33-15,33-16,33-17">Composer and the DXOS framework are open source. Due to the CRDT-based implementation there's no need for a server other than for backup. DXOS provides backup services if you want them, but you can also host your own.</cite>
- **Privacy:** <cite index="33-18">Composer is built on the peer-to-peer protocols of DXOS which are designed to be private by default.</cite>

<cite index="20-3">Composer is described as an open source "Super App" that provides teams with a fully customizable and extensible environment in a single integrated environment.</cite>

## DXOS Agents

<cite index="8-4,8-5,8-6">DXOS is a decentralized and privacy-preserving platform which communicates peer-to-peer. When all the peers go offline, it's impossible for any new peers to replicate. This is where agents come in.</cite> <cite index="8-7,8-8">Agents are just like any other peer, but are assumed to have more uptime on the network. They should typically be running on servers or desktop machines which are always plugged in.</cite>

<cite index="8-15">DXOS is also launching a fully-managed service to host agents</cite>, providing an alternative for users who don't want to run their own server infrastructure.

## Development Status

<cite index="33-4,33-5">Composer is not yet ready for production. There are bugs, and breaking changes may occur.</cite> <cite index="12-5,12-6">The DXOS SDK is under active development, with the current focus on building features and functionality that enable the development of Composer. They are currently only able to provide limited support to developers using the SDK, and will prioritize suggestions and contributions that align with work on Composer.</cite>

## Key Links

- **Website:** https://dxos.org
- **Documentation:** https://docs.dxos.org
- **GitHub:** https://github.com/dxos/dxos
- **Composer:** https://www.dxos.org/composer
- **Blog:** https://blog.dxos.org
